items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the mock-heroic register of «терзают смутные сомнения» and keeps the clipped parallelism of «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» as a comedic callback. Even though the deeper nuance (they no longer have the items) is noted in the reference analysis, the provided translation matches the approved solution exactly and is therefore treated as perfect per instructions.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It successfully reproduces the humor of Bunsha’s repetitive, banal Soviet-style politeness colliding with the royal setting, and keeps the staccato repetition that signals drunken awkwardness.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The imperative “I demand” preserves the sudden authoritarian persona shift and the comic overreach of a petty bureaucrat acting like an autocrat.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy and the blunt dismissal that creates the era-clash humor (calling her “old woman” in a modern setting).

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the subject-role of “demons” and the panicked, incantatory repetition that sells the Tsar’s supernatural interpretation of a mundane mechanical situation.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. The elevated “Behold” and “life-giving cross” maintain the religiously charged, archaic triumphal tone that is key to the joke in a modern/atheist Soviet context.

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It captures the emcee-like command and the mass-participation vibe, preserving the comic collapse of decorum and the “everyone dances” energy without sounding like a flat literalism.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It keeps the breezy self-satisfaction and opportunistic tone of Miloslavsky’s line, conveying the “lucky find” feeling in idiomatic English.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It correctly avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the Soviet-status-symbol inversion joke and the punchline rhythm with the pause before “eggplant.”

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the comic inventory cadence, the self-important “domestic” qualifier, and the trailing “Three” that signals Shpak’s escalating/exaggerated accounting.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Identical to the approved/reference translation. It keeps the calm, sing-song fatalism and the escalating inclusiveness that makes the line funny and character-consistent in the face of absurdity.

summary.interpretation: 0.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.00 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
